column7: PROLOGUS — PROLOGUE AND ARGUMENT. Lapide opens by quoting St. Jerome (Epist. ad Paulinum): "Jesus Nave, as St. Jerome says, presents a type of the Lord not only in deeds but in name: he crosses the Jordan, overthrows the kingdoms of enemies, divides the land to the victorious people, and describes — allegorically the Church, anagogically the spiritual kingdoms of the heavenly Jerusalem — through individual cities, villages, mountains, rivers, torrents and boundaries." Having completed his commentary on the Gospels, Lapide now returns with God's blessing to the Old Testament, from the antitype to the type, from Jesus to Joshua who succeeded Moses. He will be brief, even briefer than usual, since these historical books are plain, easy and pleasant and do not require as much commentary as the Prophets or Epistles. He will join the moral sense to the literal wherever found in Jerome, Augustine, Origen, Theodoret, Rabanus, and Rupert. WHY IS THE BOOK CALLED JOSHUA? Because it describes Joshua's deeds, by which he conquered Canaan and distributed it among the twelve tribes, governing the Republic of Israel with great prudence in both war and peace. God wished to give in Joshua an exemplar of the best general, leader, and prince — far superior to Xenophon's Cyrus, Plato's Republic, or Aristotle's Politics. Note on name: Originally called Hoshea ("salvation"), renamed Jehoshua ("the LORD is salvation") by Moses for his zeal and faithfulness. Son of Nun, tribe of Ephraim; devoted servant of Moses, chosen by God to govern Israel after the lawgiver's death. IS IT CANONICAL? Not only canonical but proto-canonical — the first of the Prophets in the Hebrew canon (Torah, Nebiim, Ketubim). Among the Prior Prophets they count: Joshua first, Judges second, Samuel (=1-2 Kings) third, Kings (=3-4 Kings) fourth. Reckoned as Prophets because they were written by prophets, and because the history prefigured the Messiah. WHO IS THE AUTHOR? Theodoret (Quaest. XIII), Abulensis (Quaest. V), and Andreas Masius hold it was compiled after Joshua's death from ancient diaries and annals (called "the book of the Just" in ch. 10:43) — either by Ezra (Masius) or Samuel (Abulensis). Yet the Hebrews hold Joshua to be the author, indicated by 24:26: "And he wrote all these words in the book of the law of the LORD." Certain additions were made later: the story of Achsa daughter of Caleb (ch. 15), the sons of Dan (19:47), and Joshua's own death (ch. 24). SUBJECT MATTER: To continue Moses's history through Joshua's death — especially the wars by which Joshua conquered Canaan and divided it among the tribes. The book shines with a most clear example of divine Providence: 470 years after the promise to Abraham, God brought all twelve tribes safely into the Promised Land. HOW MANY YEARS? Seven years of war plus a debated peace. Estimates vary: Rabbi Moses (peace ended with the wars); others give 10-11 years of peace (total 17-18: Genebrardus, Salianus, Torniellus, Adrichomius, Arias, Lucidus); Josephus (Antiq. V.1) gives 25; Bede and Bellarmine 26; the Fathers following the Hebrews (Eusebius, Clement of Alexandria, Augustine, Sulpicius, Magalianus, Masius) give 27. Since Joshua died at age 110 (24:29) having governed 27 years, he took command at age 83. MORAL PURPOSE: In Joshua God gives an ideal of the perfect prince. Cicero (praising Pompey) requires four qualities in a commander: military science, virtue, authority, and felicity — all of which shine wonderfully in Joshua. Tertullian (Adv. Marc. III.16) sees Joshua as the Angel-leader promised in Exod. 23. ALLEGORICAL PURPOSE: In Joshua is depicted Jesus Christ conquering sin and all the power of hell. Tertullian in verse: "The Holy Spirit joined him to himself by name... the model of the name, the image of Christ's power." TROPOLOGICAL/ANAGOGICAL PURPOSE: In Joshua is represented the way each faithful soul, like a soldier conquering not seven nations but seven capital vices, should strive victoriously toward the land of the living promised to conquerors (Origen, Theodoret, Rupert). Joshua surpassed Moses in innocence: Moses offended God at Meribah and was excluded from the Promised Land; innocent Joshua merited to lead Israel in. Nothing is blamed in Joshua; all is praised — faith, confidence, obedience, religion, charity, zeal, fortitude, integrity, justice, governance, poverty. He is in the Roman Martyrology on September 1. INTERPRETERS: Origen (36 homilies, chiefly moral); Theodoret, Procopius, Augustine (30 Questions) — literal. Later: Rupert, Lyrannus, Hugo Cardinal, Dionysius Carthusianus, Cajetanus, Alphonsus Tostatus (Abulensis, copious). Modern: Andreas Masius (excellent on geography, but occasionally carps at the Vulgate and has some less sound points on the cross and images — Molanus notes these); Arias Montanus (elegant style); from the Society: Cosmus Magalianus (copious), Nicolaus Serarius (learned), Jacobus Bonfrerius (accurate).

POST MORTEM MOYSI (After the death of Moses). Commentary: After the thirty days of mourning for Moses (Abulensis, Serarius, Cajetanus; Lyrannus, Salianus, and Masius following the Talmud disagree — see ch. 3). Since Moses died around the third of February and the Hebrews mourned thirty days, Joshua took command around the third day of Nisan; the tenth day of Nisan they crossed the Jordan (ch. 4:19); the fourteenth they celebrated Passover (ch. 5:10). "The LORD spoke" — through an Angel acting in God's place, as God had spoken to Moses through an Angel and given him the law on Sinai (Gal. 3:19; Heb. 1:14). Whenever in the Old Testament God is said to have appeared or spoken, he did so not in himself but through an Angel — as Lapide showed throughout his Pentateuch commentary. The Angel spoke to Joshua either by a spiritual locution (placing the ideas of these words in Joshua's mind) or by a bodily locution (appearing in assumed body and speaking face to face). Joshua was constituted leader immediately by God through an Angel and thereby instructed, directed, prospered with victories — whereas other kings and princes are constituted by God not immediately but through the election of peoples or nobles. St. Jerome on Tit. 1:9: "Moses, the friend of God, could well have made his sons successors in leadership and left his dignity to his posterity, but a stranger from another tribe is chosen as Jesus, so that we might know that leadership over peoples ought to be given not to blood but to quality of life. But now we see many make this a benefice, seeking not those who can profit the Church more, but those they love, or those who have bound them with services, or for whom some great man has interceded — and, to say something worse, those who by gifts obtained their ordination as clerics."

MISIT JOSUE DE SETIM DUOS VIROS EXPLORATORES IN ABSCONDITO (Joshua sent two men as spies secretly). These words are connected to ch. 1:9 and should precede v. 10 — it is a hysteron proteron, as Lapide noted there. "He sent therefore" = "he had sent therefore," i.e., before he commanded the Hebrews to prepare for crossing the Jordan in three days. Mystically (Augustine, serm. 106 De Tempore): "Jesus sends two spies because the true Jesus was to give two commandments of charity — to love God and to love neighbor. As then with provisions prepared the people came to the Jordan on the third day, so now in the Catholic Church, taking the spiritual food of faith, hope, and charity, they arrive at the mystery of the Trinity and the sacrament of Baptism." From SETIM — Shittim was in the plains of Moab near the Jordan and Mount Nebo, where Moses had died the previous month (Deut. 34). At Shittim the people had fornicated with the daughters of Moab and Midian, and there Phinehas had stabbed the Israelite with the Midianite woman (Num. 25). Shittim trees (acacia) are the lightest, smoothest, most beautiful wood, immune to decay, from which all the boards of the ark, tabernacle, and temple were made. TWO MEN — The Rabbis fable that these were Phinehas and Caleb; but who would believe that Phinehas, already designated High Priest by God, and Caleb, weighty in authority and years, would be chosen for such danger? Especially since the Septuagint (corrected at Rome) call them neaniskoi, i.e., young men, and so they are called in the Hebrew of ch. 6:23. IN ABSCONDITO — Hebrew cheres = silently, secretly (Chaldean), both so the enemy might not imprison them, and so their fellow Hebrews might not learn of it and murmur, as they had done 38 years earlier over the sending of spies; for in difficult and especially military matters, secrecy is often the key to success (Cajetanus, Masius, Serarius).

JOSUE DE NOCTE CONSURGENS MOVIT CASTRA (Joshua rose early in the morning and moved camp). Hebrew: "he went early in the morning." The Septuagint and Chaldean say "at dawn." For it is not the dark night but the luminous dawn that is fitting for moving camp and military expeditions. Let the prince and war leader learn here not to snore until late in the morning but to rise before the sun in the dawn, as Homer says: "It does not become a man of counsel to sleep the whole night." AND THEY RESTED THERE THREE DAYS — the same three days mentioned earlier, preparing for the crossing. The Hebrews hold this night was the 9th of Nisan.

TOLLANT DE MEDIO JORDANIS ALVEO DUODECIM LAPIDES (Take twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan). Not from the exact spot of the priests' feet, but from nearby. They were to carry them on their shoulders, one stone per tribe, to Gilgal, as a memorial sign. The purpose: (1) a trophy of victory over the Jordan, figuratively conquered, and over all Canaan; (2) a sign that the twelve tribes were enrolled by God as citizens and heirs of the Promised Land; (3) "that all the peoples of the earth may know the mighty hand of the LORD" (v. 24); (4) "that you also may fear the LORD your God at all times" — that you may teach your posterity to fear, love, and worship God who with such a prodigy led you into this land and will preserve you there as long as you remain in his faith and worship; when you fall away from him, he will expel you, as he did through the Babylonians and later through Titus and the Romans. Note how strictly God demands the memory of his benefits — rewarding the grateful, depriving and punishing the ungrateful. "Tomorrow" (Hebrew) signifies any future time — a synecdoche.
